h1Title: Castleman Disease: Types, Symptoms, Causes, and Treatment
heroOverview: Castleman disease is a rare lymphoproliferative disorder with two clinically distinct forms — unicentric Castleman disease, which is curable with surgical resection in over 95 percent of cases, and multicentric Castleman disease, which is life-threatening without treatment and requires systemic anti-cytokine therapy. Approximately 2–5 per million people are diagnosed annually, affecting both children and adults without significant gender predominance. The hyaline-vascular variant accounts for 90 percent of unicentric cases and presents as a single enlarged, often asymptomatic lymph node. The plasma-cell variant is more commonly multicentric, presenting with inflammatory symptoms including fevers, night sweats, weight loss, cytopenias, and organ dysfunction driven by overproduction of interleukin-6. Despite sharing the Castleman name, the disease is distinct from lymphoma.

keyStats.prevalence: 2–5 per million people diagnosed annually worldwide
keyStats.demographics: Affects adults 30–60 years for multicentric; unicentric spans all ages; slight female predominance for plasma-cell variant
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at diagnosis 50 years (multicentric), 35 years (unicentric)
keyStats.globalCases: ~6,000–7,000 new cases annually in the US; HHV-8-associated form more common in HIV-positive patients

definition: Castleman disease (ICD-10: D47.Z2) is a rare lymphoproliferative disorder characterized by benign proliferation of lymphatic tissue, first described by Benjamin Castleman in 1954. The disease is classified into unicentric (involving a single lymph node region) and multicentric (systemic, involving multiple lymph node sites) forms. Histologically, three variants exist: hyaline-vascular (the most common, accounting for ~90% of unicentric cases — characterized by regressed germinal centers, follicular dendritic cell proliferation, and increased vascularity), plasma-cell variant (characterized by sheets of plasma cells in the interfollicular zones, more commonly associated with multicentric presentation), and mixed. Multicentric Castleman disease (MCD) is driven by excessive cytokine production — particularly interleukin-6 (IL-6) — and exists in HHV-8-associated and HHV-8-negative (idiopathic) forms. HHV-8-associated MCD occurs predominantly in HIV-positive patients, driven by HHV-8-infected B-lymphocytes producing viral IL-6. Idiopathic MCD (iMCD) is a diagnosis of exclusion after ruling out HHV-8, infection, autoimmune disease, and malignancy. MCD carries a poor prognosis without treatment, with a median survival historically of 12–30 months, substantially improved with rituximab-based therapy and anti-IL-6 agents.

typesClassification.description: A single enlarged lymph node or regional lymph node station, most commonly in the chest, abdomen, or neck. Often asymptomatic and discovered incidentally. Hyaline-vascular histology in 90% of cases. Cured by complete surgical resection.

Systemic involvement of multiple lymph node sites with systemic inflammatory symptoms. Plasma-cell histology more common. Associated with HHV-8 infection (in HIV+ patients) or idiopathic. Life-threatening without treatment.

Characterized by regressive germinal centers with penetrating blood vessels (onion-skin appearance), follicular dendritic cell proliferation, and increased vascularity. Usually unicentric. May cause localized mass effect symptoms.
Characterized by sheets of polyclonal plasma cells in the interfollicular zones. Usually multicentric. Associated with elevated ESR, CRP, anemia, and hypergammaglobulinemia. More symptomatic.

primarySymptoms: Single enlarged, painless lymph node (unicentric — often discovered incidentally on imaging)
Fever and night sweats — episodic or persistent, occurring in over 80% of multicentric cases
Progressive weight loss — involuntary, exceeding 10% of baseline body weight in advanced multicentric disease
Fatigue and malaise — profound, disproportionate to activity level, driven by IL-6
Hepatosplenomegaly — enlarged liver and spleen on exam or imaging in multicentric disease
Peripheral edema and ascites — from capillary leak syndrome and hypoalbuminemia in severe MCD
Skin rash with violaceous papules or plaques — characteristic of HHV-8-associated MCD
Anemia (normocytic normochromic) — from chronic inflammation and hemophagocytosis
Elevated inflammatory markers — ESR and CRP markedly elevated, often >100 mg/L ESR
Neurologic symptoms in TAFRO syndrome — thrombocytopenia, anasarca, fever, reticulin fibrosis of bone marrow, organomegaly
earlyWarningSigns: A new persistent lymph node enlargement, especially if accompanied by fever or B symptoms
Unexplained anemia with normal or elevated platelets — think MCD
Recurrent fevers without infectious source despite negative workup
Markedly elevated ESR (>50 mm/hr) in a patient with lymphadenopathy
HIV-positive patient with new-onset lymphadenopathy and systemic symptoms
emergencySigns: Rapidly enlarging lymph nodes with compression symptoms — possible airway compromise (mediastinal nodes)
Severe infection (sepsis) in setting of immunosuppression from MCD or its treatment
Acute kidney injury with proteinuria — possible POEMS syndrome overlap or renal amyloidosis
Worsening cytopenias with fever — possible hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH)
Neurologic deterioration in TAFRO syndrome — encephalopathy, seizures

causes.description: Kaposi's sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (HHV-8) is found in the neoplastic B cells in ~50% of HIV-associated MCD cases and a smaller proportion of HIV-negative MCD cases. HHV-8 encodes viral IL-6 (vIL-6) which drives B-cell proliferation and the plasma-cell infiltration characteristic of this form.

In HHV-8-negative MCD (iMCD), the pathogenesis involves dysregulated overproduction of host IL-6 and other pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-1, TNF-alpha, and VEGF. Genetic polymorphisms in IL-6 promoter regions and autoimmune associations support an immune-dysregulatory mechanism.

MCD can occur as part of POEMS syndrome (Polyneuropathy, Organomegaly, Endocrinopathy, M-protein, Skin changes), a plasma cell dyscrasia with paraprotein production. POEMS-associated MCD is driven by underlying plasma cell neoplasm and requires treatment targeting the plasma cell clone.

Activating mutations in JAK2 (present in ~30% of iMCD cases) lead to constitutive activation of the JAK-STAT signaling pathway, driving IL-6 independent cell proliferation. JAK2 V617F is the most common mutation found in iMCD-NOS.

In a significant proportion of iMCD cases, no clear etiologic driver is identified. Current models propose that an unknown infectious trigger, autoimmune stimulus, or genetic predisposition leads to a self-perpetuating inflammatory cytokine cascade centered on IL-6.

Tumor-associated immune responses in underlying solid malignancies (lung, gastric, renal) may occasionally manifest as a paraneoplastic MCD-like syndrome, with systemic inflammatory symptoms that resolve with treatment of the primary malignancy.

riskFactors.description: 15-fold increased risk of MCD, HHV-8 co-infection drives pathogenesis
slight male predominance in plasma-cell variant
higher incidence of MCD in patients with RA, SLE, and autoimmune cytopenias

affectedDemographics: Incidence estimated at 2–5 per million people annually; no significant gender predominance overall, though plasma-cell variant shows slight female predominance
Median age at diagnosis 50 years for multicentric, 35 years for unicentric; range spans children to elderly
HHV-8-associated MCD disproportionately affects HIV-positive patients — 15-fold increased risk compared to HIV-negative individuals (CDC data)
Asian-Pacific populations show higher rates of TAFRO syndrome variant, suggesting possible genetic or environmental modifiers
No established familial clustering pattern; JAK2 V617F is an acquired mutation, not hereditary
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing Castleman disease requires distinguishing unicentric from multicentric forms, as management differs fundamentally. For suspected unicentric disease, a contrast-enhanced CT or PET-CT identifies the dominant nodal mass and surgical excision is both diagnostic and therapeutic. Histology is definitive: hyaline-vascular variant shows regressed germinal centers with penetrating blood vessels (onion-skin appearance), while plasma-cell variant shows interfollicular plasma cell sheets. For multicentric disease, diagnosis is clinical and laboratory: lymphadenopathy at multiple sites, systemic inflammatory symptoms, cytopenias, hypoalbuminemia, elevated CRP and ESR, and organ dysfunction. HIV testing is mandatory and HHV-8 viral load testing should be performed in all cases. In HHV-8-negative patients, autoimmune workup (ANA, rheumatoid factor) and FDG-PET scanning help exclude lymphoma and other lymphoproliferative disorders before confirming idiopathic MCD. Lymph node biopsy confirms diagnosis and differentiates HHV-8-driven from idiopathic MCD. Bone marrow biopsy is indicated when there is concern for concurrent B-cell lymphoma. Serum IL-6 levels may be elevated in idiopathic MCD and are used in some diagnostic algorithms.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard diagnostic procedure — confirms Castleman histology and excludes lymphoma and metastatic cancer
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Day-case or overnight surgery under general anesthesia. Pathology takes 3–7 days. Immunohistochemistry for HHV-8 LANA-1 and CD138 is standard.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes HHV-8-associated MCD from idiopathic MCD — critical for treatment selection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw, quantitative PCR. Positive result confirms HHV-8-associated MCD. Results in 3–5 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Staging assessment for lymphadenopathy distribution — distinguishes unicentric from multicentric disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient scan, ~45 minutes. Shows metabolically active nodes (SUV typically 4–8 in Castleman nodes). Also detects occult sites.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated in ~70% of iMCD cases; correlates with disease activity; used to monitor treatment response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw. IL-6 levels >20 pg/mL support iMCD diagnosis. May be normal in HHV-8-associated MCD.

diagnosticTests.test: Bone marrow biopsy with reticular fibrosis grading
diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates for POEMS syndrome (plasma cell neoplasm) and TAFRO syndrome (fibrosis grading)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy under local anesthesia. Results in 5–7 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rules out autoimmune disease mimics and HIV co-infection which changes management
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw. HIV antibody/antigen combo test plus autoimmune serologies. Results in 3–5 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment diverges sharply by form. Unicentric Castleman disease is cured by complete surgical resection, achievable laparoscopically in most cases, with greater than 95 percent disease-free survival at 5 years. When complete surgical resection is not feasible due to anatomical location in the mediastinum or retroperitoneum, debulking surgery or involved-field radiotherapy is used and outcomes remain excellent for most patients. Multicentric Castleman disease requires systemic therapy. First-line treatment is anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody rituximab, which produces response rates of 60 to 80 percent and substantially improves survival in HHV-8-positive MCD. For severe or rituximab-refractory disease, the anti-IL-6 siltuximab (FDA-approved 2014 for iMCD) or the anti-IL-6 receptor antibody tocilizumab are highly effective, reducing symptoms and inflammatory markers. Siltuximab 11 mg per kg every 3 weeks produces durable responses in iMCD. JAK2 inhibitors such as ruxolitinib have shown promise in iMCD patients with JAK2 V617F mutations. Corticosteroids are used as a bridge in acute severe disease. Watchful waiting is appropriate for minimally symptomatic unicentric disease not amenable to complete resection, with serial imaging surveillance every 6 to 12 months. Patients with HIV-associated multicentric disease require specialist coordination with infectious disease. Long-term follow-up with CT or PET imaging is recommended for all MCD patients to monitor for lymphoma transformation, which occurs in approximately 10 to 15 percent of cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab (Rituxan) ± chemotherapy
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody targeting B cells driving the plasma-cell infiltration. Given as 375 mg/m² weekly for 4 weeks, with or without CHOP chemotherapy. Achieves partial or complete remission in 60–90% of MCD patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Overall response rate 60–90%; median duration of response 17–34 months. Rituximab alone is less toxic and preferred in less severe disease. Steroid-free regimens reduce infection risk.

medicalTreatments.name: Siltuximab (Sylvant)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-IL-6 monoclonal antibody that binds circulating IL-6, blocking its pro-inflammatory signaling. FDA-approved specifically for iMCD. Given as 11 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Phase 2 randomized trial: 34% response rate vs 0% for placebo by prespecified criteria; durable responses maintained over 5+ years in responders.

medicalTreatments.name: Tocilizumab (Actemra)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-IL-6 receptor monoclonal antibody. Used off-label for both HHV-8-associated MCD and iMCD. Dosed at 8 mg/kg IV every 2–4 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Retrospective series show clinical response rates of 50–70%; effective in TAFRO syndrome specifically.

medicalTreatments.name: Ruxolitinib (Jakafi)
medicalTreatments.description: JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor targeting the constitutively active JAK-STAT pathway in JAK2-mutated iMCD. Oral dosing 5–20 mg twice daily.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case series in JAK2 V617F+ iMCD: symptom improvement in 60%; spleen reduction in 40–50%; still investigational for MCD.

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids (prednisone)
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose corticosteroids (prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day) provide symptomatic relief through broad anti-inflammatory effects in acute MCD flares.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom control in ~40–60% during treatment; no durable response; steroid taper often leads to relapse.

medicalTreatments.name: Chemotherapy (CHOP or CVP)
medicalTreatments.description: Cytotoxic chemotherapy used in combination with rituximab for aggressive or refractory MCD. Cyclophosphamide + doxorubicin + vincristine + prednisone with rituximab (R-CHOP) is most common.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: High response rates in severe MCD but significant toxicity — reserved for life-threatening presentations or rituximab-refractory disease.

surgicalOptions.procedure: Complete surgical resection of affected lymph node
surgicalOptions.description: Curative for unicentric Castleman disease. Complete removal of the dominant lymph node and any visibly involved adjacent nodes. Performed via thoracotomy (mediastinal), laparotomy (abdominal), or open approach depending on location.
surgicalOptions.indications: Unicentric Castleman disease — any surgically accessible site. Also diagnostic for unclear cases.

surgicalOptions.description: Partial removal of dominant lymph node masses in patients with compressive symptoms from unicentric or unresectable disease. Reduces cytokine load and relieves mass effect.
surgicalOptions.indications: Symptomatic unicentric disease where complete resection is not possible due to anatomic constraints.

surgicalOptions.description: Focused radiation to residual or unresectable unicentric Castleman disease deposits. Useful when surgery is not feasible.
surgicalOptions.indications: Unresectable unicentric disease, residual disease post-surgery, or medically frail patients unable to tolerate surgery.

alternativeTreatments.name: Omega-3 fatty acid supplementation (fish oil 2–4 g daily)
alternativeTreatments.description: Modest anti-inflammatory effect through reduction in IL-1 and TNF-alpha production. Observational studies in chronic inflammatory conditions suggest symptom benefit; not a substitute for anti-IL-6 therapy in active MCD.

prognosis: Unicentric Castleman disease carries an excellent prognosis after complete surgical resection — 5-year survival exceeds 95 percent, with most patients experiencing permanent cure. Recurrence is rare unless resection was incomplete. Multicentric Castleman disease prognosis has improved substantially with the advent of rituximab-based therapy: median survival has increased from 12–30 months (pre-rituximab era) to more than 50 months in contemporary series. With modern anti-cytokine therapy (rituximab, siltuximab, tocilizumab), many patients achieve sustained remission with ongoing maintenance therapy. However, MCD remains a chronic relapsing condition — most patients require long-term or indefinite treatment to maintain remission. TAFRO syndrome carries a particularly guarded prognosis with high rates of renal failure and early mortality. HHV-8-associated MCD in HIV-positive patients also carries worse outcomes related to concurrent opportunistic infections.
preventionStrategies: No proven primary prevention exists for idiopathic Castleman disease
HIV management with antiretroviral therapy — reduces HHV-8-associated MCD risk in HIV+ patients
Early recognition of constitutional symptoms in patients with known HIV or prior malignancy
Avoidance of immunosuppressive medications that could reactivate HHV-8 in seropositive patients
Surveillance imaging (every 6–12 months) in patients with treated MCD to detect early relapse
lifestyleModifications: Moderate exercise (150 min/week) during remission — improves fatigue and functional status
Balanced diet high in protein (1.2 g/kg/day) — counteracts catabolic state and supports immune function
Infection precautions during immunosuppressive treatment — hand hygiene, food safety, avoidance of crowds
Stress management and mental health support — living with a rare disease causes significant psychological burden
Smoking cessation — smoking amplifies inflammatory cytokine milieu
Regular follow-up with hematology/oncology every 3–6 months during active disease, every 6–12 months in remission

recommended: High-protein diet to counteract inflammatory catabolism and support immune cell production
Iron-rich foods (lean red meat, beans, fortified cereals) to counteract anemia of chronic disease
Anti-inflammatory foods: fatty fish (salmon, sardines rich in omega-3), turmeric, green leafy vegetables
Vitamin D supplementation (1000–4000 IU daily) — often deficient in chronic inflammatory disease and supports immune regulation
Small frequent meals during periods of active disease-related anorexia and early satiety from organomegaly
avoid: Alcohol — worsens liver involvement in MCD and interferes with medication metabolism
Raw or undercooked foods during neutropenia from chemotherapy — infection risk
Excessive caffeine — may exacerbate anxiety and insomnia in MCD patients on steroids
Grapefruit juice — CYP3A4 interactions with siltuximab and many chemotherapeutic agents
Highly processed foods with pro-inflammatory omega-6 fatty acid burden (vegetable oils, fried foods)

complications: Infections from immunosuppression — hypogammaglobulinemia and neutropenia from both disease and treatment increase serious bacterial, viral, and fungal infection risk
Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) — life-threatening hyperinflammatory syndrome with erythrophagocytosis (macrophages engulfing red blood cells) and cytokine storm that can complicate MCD
Malignant transformation — patients with MCD have an increased risk of developing B-cell lymphomas (particularly primary effusion lymphoma in HHV-8+ cases) and Kaposi sarcoma
Renal failure — can result from MCD-associated amyloidosis (AA amyloidosis) or TAFRO syndrome with renal thrombotic microangiopathy
Persistent fatigue and deconditioning — even in remission, many MCD survivors experience reduced exercise tolerance and chronic fatigue lasting months to years

faqs.question: Is Castleman disease a type of cancer?
faqs.answer: Castleman disease is classified as a lymphoproliferative disorder rather than a true malignancy. Unicentric disease is benign and curable with surgery. Multicentric disease is more aggressive but also not a classical cancer — it does not metastasize in the way lymphoma does. However, MCD increases the risk of developing lymphoma and other hematologic malignancies, so long-term monitoring is essential.

faqs.question: What is the difference between unicentric and multicentric Castleman disease?
faqs.answer: Unicentric Castleman disease involves a single lymph node or regional group and is usually curable with surgery. Multicentric Castleman disease involves multiple lymph node sites throughout the body with systemic inflammatory symptoms and is life-threatening without treatment.

faqs.question: How is Castleman disease diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires excisional lymph node biopsy showing characteristic Castleman histology. For multicentric disease, full-body PET-CT and HHV-8 testing are performed to stage the extent and determine the HHV-8 status. Blood tests showing elevated inflammatory markers and anemia support the diagnosis.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for unicentric Castleman disease?
faqs.answer: Complete surgical resection of the affected lymph node is curative in over 95 percent of unicentric Castleman disease cases. If complete resection is not possible due to anatomic location, debulking surgery or radiation therapy can reduce symptoms.

faqs.question: What medications treat multicentric Castleman disease?
faqs.answer: The first-line treatment for MCD is rituximab (anti-CD20 antibody), often combined with chemotherapy. For rituximab-refractory idiopathic MCD, siltuximab (anti-IL-6 antibody) is FDA-approved. Tocilizumab and ruxolitinib are alternative options for refractory disease.

faqs.question: What is siltuximab used for in Castleman disease?
faqs.answer: Siltuximab (Sylvant) is an anti-IL-6 monoclonal antibody approved specifically for idiopathic multicentric Castleman disease. It binds circulating IL-6, blocking the cytokine that drives the plasma-cell infiltration and inflammatory symptoms. It is given as an IV infusion every 3 weeks.

faqs.question: Can Castleman disease come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Unicentric Castleman disease rarely recurs after complete surgical resection. Multicentric Castleman disease is a chronic relapsing condition — most patients require ongoing or maintenance therapy to sustain remission. Rituximab maintenance every 2–3 months or continuous siltuximab therapy are used to prevent relapse.

faqs.question: What is TAFRO syndrome?
faqs.answer: TAFRO is a variant of Castleman disease characterized by thrombocytopenia, anasarca (generalized edema), fever, reticulin fibrosis of the bone marrow, and organomegaly. It carries a particularly poor prognosis with progressive renal failure and is often resistant to standard rituximab therapy.

faqs.question: Is Castleman disease hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most cases of Castleman disease are sporadic and not inherited. However, JAK2 V617F mutations are found in approximately 30 percent of idiopathic MCD cases — these are acquired mutations in blood cells, not germline changes. No clearly established hereditary pattern exists, though familial clustering has been reported rarely.

faqs.question: How common is Castleman disease?
faqs.answer: Castleman disease is rare — approximately 2–5 per million people are diagnosed annually, meaning roughly 6,000–7,000 new cases per year in the United States. Multicentric disease accounts for about 30 percent of cases, with unicentric being more common.

faqs.question: What causes Castleman disease?
faqs.answer: In HHV-8-associated MCD, infection with Kaposi's sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (HHV-8) drives B-cell proliferation through viral IL-6 production. In idiopathic MCD, the cause is unclear but involves dysregulated host IL-6 production, JAK2 mutations in some cases, and autoimmune associations.

faqs.question: Can Castleman disease be cured?
faqs.answer: Unicentric Castleman disease can be cured with complete surgical resection — cure rates exceed 95 percent with complete removal of the affected node. Multicentric Castleman disease is not currently curable but is highly treatable: most patients achieve sustained remission with modern anti-cytokine therapy and can live normal lives on ongoing treatment.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy with Castleman disease?
faqs.answer: For unicentric disease with successful surgery, life expectancy is normal. For multicentric disease, median survival has improved from 12–30 months in the pre-rituximab era to more than 50 months with modern treatment. With current anti-IL-6 therapies, many patients achieve durable remission extending decades.

faqs.question: Is Castleman disease related to HIV?
faqs.answer: HIV infection increases the risk of HHV-8-associated MCD by approximately 15-fold through immunosuppression that allows HHV-8 reactivation. HIV+ patients with MCD typically have more aggressive disease and higher relapse rates. Effective antiretroviral therapy is essential alongside MCD-directed treatment.

faqs.question: What does a Castleman disease biopsy show?
faqs.answer: The histopathology shows enlarged germinal centers with characteristic 'onion-skin' appearance (concentric layers of lymphocytes around regressed germinal centers) and increased vascularity in hyaline-vascular disease. Plasma-cell variant shows sheets of mature plasma cells in the interfollicular zones. Immunohistochemistry for HHV-8 LANA-1 confirms viral association.

faqs.question: How is Castleman disease different from lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Castleman disease is classified as a lymphoproliferative disorder of uncertain malignant potential, distinct from lymphoma. Unlike lymphoma, Castleman disease cells are polyclonal (not malignant clones) and do not metastasize in the classical sense. However, MCD increases the risk of developing lymphoma, so patients require long-term monitoring.

faqs.question: What is the role of IL-6 in Castleman disease?
faqs.answer: IL-6 is the central cytokine driver of Castleman disease. Overproduction of IL-6 by B cells and stromal cells causes the characteristic plasma-cell infiltration, systemic inflammation (fever, fatigue, weight loss), hepatosplenomegaly, and laboratory abnormalities (anemia, elevated ESR). Anti-IL-6 therapies like siltuximab and tocilizumab directly target this pathway.

faqs.question: Does Castleman disease affect children?
faqs.answer: Unicentric Castleman disease occurs in both children and adults. Multicentric disease is rare in children but does occur, particularly in association with HIV and HHV-8 in endemic regions. Pediatric cases require specialized care at tertiary centers.

faqs.question: Can you live a normal life with Castleman disease?
faqs.answer: After treatment for unicentric disease, most patients return to completely normal health. For multicentric disease, many patients achieve sustained remission with ongoing treatment and can maintain normal work, relationships, and quality of life. Fatigue may persist even in remission, and regular monitoring is required.

faqs.question: What happens if Castleman disease is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Unicentric disease causes only a painless mass if untreated — but the mass can compress adjacent structures (airway, vessels, nerves). Multicentric disease left untreated progresses to severe cachexia, infections from immunosuppression, organ failure (renal, hepatic), and death within 12–30 months on average.

confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lymphoma shows malignant monoclonal B-cell or T-cell infiltration on biopsy with architectural effacement; Castleman disease shows reactive follicular hyperplasia with polyclonal plasma cells and characteristic vascular changes
SLE causes autoimmune inflammation with positive ANA, anti-dsDNA antibodies, and characteristic multi-organ involvement; Castleman disease is defined by lymph node histology showing specific patterns
EBV mononucleosis causes acute febrile illness with atypical lymphocytosis, positive heterophile antibody test, and resolves within weeks; Castleman disease is chronic and confirmed by biopsy

whySeeSpecialist: Patients with Castleman disease should be referred to a hematologist or oncologist with expertise in rare lymphoproliferative disorders — self-referral or GP management of MCD is associated with delayed diagnosis and worse outcomes. Seek specialist care urgently if systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss, cytopenias) develop, if lymph nodes are rapidly enlarging, or if HHV-8 or HIV co-infection is confirmed, as these require specific treatment protocols. Unicentric disease can be managed by a general surgeon or thoracic surgeon with lymphoma surgery experience once diagnosis is established.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a hematologist or oncologist affiliated with a major cancer center with a dedicated lymphoproliferative disorders program — these centers see higher volumes of MCD and have access to siltuximab, JAK2 inhibitor trials, and multidisciplinary support. For unicentric disease, a surgical specialist (cardiothoracic, abdominal, or ENT depending on node location) with experience in lymph node resection is critical, as complete resection determines cure. Ask the specialist how many Castleman disease patients they have managed in the past year; a volume of five or more annually suggests appropriate expertise.
exerciseGuidelines: During active MCD with systemic symptoms — fever, fatigue, cytopenias — rest and avoid strenuous exercise. During remission or for stable unicentric disease, moderate aerobic exercise (walking, cycling, swimming) for 30–45 minutes most days is safe and helps combat fatigue and deconditioning. Avoid heavy resistance training during corticosteroid therapy due to infection risk and steroid myopathy. Avoid contact sports during neutropenia from chemotherapy. Listen to your body — persistent post-exercise exhaustion beyond baseline fatigue warrants discussion with your hematologist.
dailyManagement: Adhere to treatment schedules — rituximab infusions, siltuximab IV every 3 weeks, or tocilizumab every 2–4 weeks; gaps in treatment increase relapse risk in MCD
Monitor temperature daily during active disease and immunosuppressed periods — fever above 38C warrants urgent evaluation for infection
Track weight weekly — unexplained loss exceeding 5% warrants review; early satiety from organomegaly can mask disease progression
Report new lymph node enlargement or persistent swelling (face, hands, ankles) to your hematologist promptly, as these may signal relapse
Maintain updated medication list including allopurinol status if using corticosteroids or chemotherapy — carry this for any emergency visit
Schedule dental check-ups every 6 months during immunosuppressive therapy; neutropenia and immunosuppression increase infection risk
recoveryTimeline: Unicentric Castleman disease patients typically recover fully within 4–8 weeks after complete surgical resection, with no long-term management needed beyond routine follow-up imaging. Multicentric disease recovery is more gradual: initial responses to rituximab or anti-IL-6 therapy are often seen within 2–4 weeks (fever resolves, energy improves, lymph nodes shrink), but full laboratory normalization — CRP normalization, anemia correction — takes 2–3 months. Maintenance rituximab every 2–3 months or continuous siltuximab is typically required indefinitely; attempts to discontinue are associated with relapse in the majority of patients. TAFRO syndrome recovery is slower and less complete; many patients remain on chronic dialysis or have persistent proteinuria even with disease remission.

supportResources.name: Castleman Disease Network (CDCN)
supportResources.description: Patient-run organization funding research, maintaining the Castleman disease clinical registry, and connecting patients with experts. Operates the largest CD database in the world.

supportResources.name: Lymphoma Research Foundation
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient education resources on lymphoproliferative disorders including Castleman disease; nurse helpline and clinical trial matching available.

supportResources.name: NIH National Cancer Institute — Castleman Disease
supportResources.description: Authoritative NCI page on Castleman disease subtypes, treatment approaches, and ongoing clinical trials.

supportResources.name: CDCN Physician Network
supportResources.description: Directory of Castleman disease specialists compiled by the Castleman Disease Collaborative Network for specialist referrals.

simpleName: Castleman Disease (rare lymph node disorder with two distinct forms — single-site and systemic)
metaTitle: Castleman Disease: Types, Treatment & Causes | aihealz
metaDescription: Castleman disease: rare lymphoproliferative disorder. Unicentric vs multicentric types, symptoms, rituximab, siltuximab treatment, and prognosis reviewed.
keywords: Castleman disease, multicentric Castleman disease, unicentric Castleman disease, HHV-8, IL-6, siltuximab, rituximab, lymphoma, lymphadenopathy, plasma cell
